summary: I came to Los Angeles in 1927, and I've been here for nearly fifty years. Unlike other metropolitan areas, the art world in Los Angeles was completely different because the city had no real center. It was made up of many villages spreading out like a cancer, with business districts scattered and constantly changing. This unique layout affected how artists lived and worked here compared to places like New York or San Francisco.

San Francisco reminded me a lot of New York or London when it came to the art scene. Artists there tended to cluster in specific neighborhoods, with successful ones living in one area and mavericks in another, often in older, less desirable parts that they would renovate. This pattern was familiar and similar to what happened in places like Greenwich Village. The social dynamics of artists in San Francisco were much more traditional compared to Los Angeles.
cues: similar to New York and London

summary: New York City, in my experience, had a centralized downtown district that was somewhat like a smaller version of what you might find in Los Angeles. The art world there was more structured, with artists living in distinct areas based on their success and style. The social aspect of being an artist was important, much like it was for professionals in Beverly Hills. Compared to Los Angeles, New York had a more traditional and concentrated urban layout.

London's art scene resembled that of San Francisco and New York, with artists grouped in particular neighborhoods. There was a clear social structure where successful artists lived in fashionable areas, while mavericks occupied older, less desirable districts that they revitalized. This pattern was quite common in older cities with established centers, unlike the sprawling and decentralized nature of Los Angeles. London represented the traditional metropolitan art environment I was familiar with before moving west.

Greenwich Village was a classic example of how artists would move into once respectable neighborhoods that had fallen out of favor, fix up old houses, and eventually be pushed out as the area became fashionable again. This cycle was typical in many cities, including San Francisco and New York. I saw this pattern as a social and economic reality for artists trying to establish themselves. It contrasted sharply with the situation in Los Angeles, where such clear neighborhoods didn't really exist.

Beverly Hills represented the high end of the social spectrum, much like the successful artists who wanted to show their status to get better commissions. Being located in Beverly Hills was like a doctor or lawyer charging different prices based on their prestigious address. It was a place where social standing and location directly influenced professional success. This was quite different from the more dispersed and less status-driven art world I found in Los Angeles.

Ward Montague was a sculptor friend of mine who, around 1928, was doing stonecarving work at the Bullocks Wilshire building. His involvement in that project was notable because Bullocks Wilshire was considered quite a folly at the time. I remember mentioning him to give a sense of how the city and its art scene were evolving during those years. Ward's work connected me to the early days of Los Angeles' development and its unique architectural projects.

Bullocks Wilshire was a building under construction around 1928 where my friend Ward Montague was doing stonecarving work. At that time, the project was considered a folly, reflecting the ambitious and sometimes risky developments happening in Los Angeles. The building symbolized the city's growth and the changing landscape that artists and others were part of. It stood as a marker of the city's evolving character during those early years.
